behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.explanation: Hyaline/articular cartilage is predominantly type II collagen; type I is bone/tendon, type X is hypertrophic growth-plate cartilage.

options.C: Appearing with degenerative disease in old age

questions.explanation: Seronegative (RF-negative) spondyloarthropathies (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic, reactive) are HLA-B27–linked and preferentially involve the axial skeleton and sacroiliac joints.

questions.stem: Cartilage's compressive resistance/elasticity is due to
questions.explanation: Negatively-charged GAGs (aggrecan) osmotically draw in water, giving cartilage its swelling pressure and compressive resilience; cartilage is avascular.

questions.stem: A cause of prerenal AKI may be

options.A: Severe nephrotic syndrome with marked hypoalbuminaemia

questions.explanation: Hypoalbuminaemia lowers oncotic pressure → ↓effective circulating volume (prerenal). The others cause intrinsic injury (AIN/ATN).

options.A: Multiple myeloma is a characteristic case of overflow proteinuria

questions.explanation: Nephrotic-range is >3.5 g/24h; a small amount of albumin is normally excreted.

options.D: Obstruction always distal to pelviureteric junction

questions.explanation: Relieving obstruction causes post-obstructive polyuria (osmotic diuresis + tubular ADH resistance); the risk is hypovolaemia, not hyperkalaemia.
Stage 3a (GFR 45–59) is usually asymptomatic; uraemic features appear in stages 4–5.

options.A: Creatinine replaced by cystatin C in practice
options.D: Prolonged prerenal AKI may progress to ATN

questions.explanation: Sustained hypoperfusion converts reversible prerenal azotaemia into ischaemic ATN; creatinine is not replaced by cystatin C, and non-oliguric AKI is common.
As GFR falls serum creatinine rises (inverse relationship); creatinine-based CKD-EPI/MDRD are preferred and urea clearance is unreliable.

questions.stem: The endocrine role of the kidneys includes
questions.explanation: Kidney does 1α-hydroxylation; 25-hydroxylation is hepatic. PTH is parathyroid.
ADH half-life is ~15–20 min.
Men have a higher body-water percentage (more muscle, less fat); total body water ≈60% and about two-thirds is intracellular.
Failing kidneys cannot excrete K⁺; loop diuretics and β-agonists lower K⁺, and hyperaldosteronism causes hypokalaemia.

options.A: In diarrhoea renal K excretion >50 mEq/day

questions.explanation: Aldosterone drives renal K⁺ wasting → hypokalaemia; K-sparing diuretics raise K⁺ and β-blockers tend to raise it too.

questions.stem: Causes of type 2 respiratory failure do NOT include

options.C: Stimulation of respiratory centre e.g. progesterone

questions.explanation: Keyed: stimulation of the respiratory centre causes hyper-, not hypo-ventilation. Note: pneumonia (parenchymal) causes type 1 failure, so it's also defensibly 'not type 2' — examiner-dependent.
Hypercapnia (type 2 failure) reflects alveolar hypoventilation; shunt, low V/Q and low FiO₂ cause hypoxaemia without early CO₂ retention.

questions.stem: Weakness of upper-airway muscles does NOT cause
questions.explanation: Weak upper-airway muscles impair swallowing/speech and predispose to aspiration and infection — 'adequate speech' is the outcome that would NOT occur.
COPD is a disease of older heavy smokers (>20–30 pack-years) with a low FEV1/FVC and minimal bronchodilator reversibility (unlike asthma).
Restrictive fibrosis reduces all volumes proportionally (low TLC, low FRC), so the FEV1/FVC ratio is preserved or increased.
The others all cause hypoventilation.

options.A: Upper regions hyperventilated relative to perfusion
options.B: Upper regions hypoventilated relative to perfusion
options.C: Perfusion at bases double that at apices
options.D: Perfusion at bases half that at apices

questions.explanation: At the apex ventilation exceeds perfusion (high V/Q); perfusion is greatest at the bases, roughly the reverse of the false options.

questions.stem: Severe heart failure with pleural effusion → expect
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic effusions are transudates (high hydrostatic pressure, low protein) and are typically bilateral.

questions.stem: Hypoventilation is observed in all EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Reducing dead space improves ventilation; muscle fatigue, relaxants and CNS lesions all cause hypoventilation.

questions.stem: A cause of malabsorption in the mucosal phase is NOT
questions.explanation: Ischaemia impairs the transport/delivery phase; coeliac, parasites and Crohn's damage the mucosa itself.

questions.stem: A cause of malabsorption in the mucosal phase
questions.explanation: Coeliac disease destroys villous mucosa (mucosal phase); Zollinger-Ellison and bacterial overgrowth are intraluminal, ischaemia is transport-phase.

options.A: Acid secretion usually normal or decreased
options.D: H. pylori is not a cause

questions.explanation: Gastric ulcers arise from impaired mucosal defence (acid often normal/low), can be malignant, and H. pylori is a major cause.
Achalasia is a neuronal disorder (loss of myenteric inhibitory neurons) with failure of LES relaxation. By elimination the manometric 'simultaneous/spontaneous contractions' is the keyed answer.
Secretory diarrhoea is large-volume, persists during fasting, and has a low stool osmotic gap (active ion secretion).

questions.stem: In the pathogenesis of ascites in cirrhosis

options.B: CO is the main vasodilation mediator

questions.explanation: Portal HTN is required; NO is the main vasodilator; Na-retaining systems are activated.
Unabsorbed solutes (e.g. disaccharidase/lactase deficiency) draw water in; osmotic diarrhoea stops with fasting and has a high stool osmotic gap.

options.B: May lead to hypovolaemia and shock
options.D: Acute pancreatitis is among its causes

questions.explanation: Proximal/vomiting → alkalosis; distal → acidosis (the a/c options are swapped).

options.D: Promote bile flow + secretion of phospholipids/cholesterol/bilirubin

questions.explanation: Bile acids are synthesised from cholesterol, undergo enterohepatic recirculation, are essential for fat and fat-soluble vitamin absorption, and are choleretic.

options.B: Produces PGI2, the body's most potent vasodilator
options.D: Does not vary along the arterial tree

questions.explanation: Intact endothelium releases NO in response to acetylcholine (vasodilation); it is antithrombotic and its properties vary along the arterial tree.

questions.stem: Endothelial dysfunction is more problematic in
questions.explanation: Examiner-dependent. Keyed to unstable angina (dysfunction → vasospasm/plaque instability); some sources emphasise it as the earliest lesion in hyperlipidaemia.

questions.stem: Coronary flow reserve is significantly affected when stenosis exceeds
questions.explanation: Commonly taught threshold ~70%. (Resting flow is affected only at ~90%; some texts cite 50% for early reserve loss.)

questions.stem: Most common haemorrhagic manifestation of haemophilia A
questions.explanation: Factor VIII deficiency causes deep bleeding into joints (haemarthrosis) and muscles rather than mucosal bleeding.

options.B: Urine Na may be > or < 20 depending on cause

questions.explanation: Renal salt loss gives urine Na >20; extrarenal loss (GI, sweat) gives avid retention with urine Na <20.

questions.stem: Main mechanism of dyspnoea in heart failure
questions.explanation: Raised LVEDP transmits back to the pulmonary capillaries → interstitial/alveolar congestion and dyspnoea.

questions.stem: An atherosclerotic plaque is unstable when there is
questions.explanation: Vulnerable plaques have a large lipid core and a thin fibrous cap prone to rupture; a thick cap with small core is stable.
ADH deficiency or resistance produces large volumes of dilute urine with compensatory polydipsia; vasopressin is low in central DI.
Circulating platelets survive about 7–10 days.
The exogenous (dietary) pathway carries chylomicron lipids from gut to tissues; the other options are glucose/metabolic pathways.

questions.stem: Main clinical manifestations of heart failure
questions.explanation: Low output and pulmonary congestion produce fatigue and exertional breathlessness as the cardinal symptoms.

questions.stem: Which is an acquired bleeding disorder
questions.explanation: DIC is acquired; haemophilia A/B and von Willebrand disease are inherited.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a clinical sign of heart failure
questions.explanation: JVD, oedema and basal crackles are congestive signs; hypotension is a late/shock feature, not a defining sign of heart failure.

questions.stem: Which acts as an inhibitor of haematopoiesis
questions.explanation: TNF (and interferons) suppress haematopoiesis; EPO, TPO and G-CSF are stimulatory growth factors.
The hallmark is loss of nitric-oxide bioavailability, with increased adhesion molecules and a pro-thrombotic, pro-inflammatory shift.

questions.stem: Hepcidin is important in the pathogenesis of
questions.explanation: Inflammation raises hepcidin, trapping iron in stores and macrophages → the iron-restricted anaemia of chronic disease.

questions.stem: Which are NOT mature blood cells
questions.explanation: Myelocytes are immature granulocyte precursors; RBCs and neutrophils are mature circulating cells.

questions.stem: The Mantoux test is an example of
questions.explanation: The tuberculin skin test is a delayed, T-cell-mediated type IV reaction peaking at 48–72h.

questions.stem: A cause of decreased ventilation is NOT

options.A: Stimulation of respiratory centre e.g. progesterone

questions.explanation: Keyed: stimulation increases ventilation. Note: pneumonia causes type 1 (hypoxaemic) failure rather than hypoventilation — also defensible.
Angiotensin II stimulates thirst (and ADH), causes vasoconstriction, sympathetic activation and sodium retention — not natriuresis or vasodilation.

questions.stem: The direct Coombs test is positive in
questions.explanation: The direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test detects antibody/complement coating RBCs, positive in autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a cause of heart failure
questions.explanation: Athlete's heart is a physiological adaptation; CAD, dilated cardiomyopathy, valve disease and hypertension all cause heart failure.
Classic type 1 diabetes autoantibodies are anti-islet (ICA), anti-insulin (IAA) and anti-GAD65 (also IA-2/ZnT8).

questions.stem: An initial finding in acute PE is NOT
questions.explanation: Acute PE causes tachypnoea → hypocapnia and respiratory alkalosis with hypoxaemia; acidosis is not the early picture.
Memory (faster, stronger secondary responses) is a defining feature of adaptive, not innate, immunity.
Deep, rapid Kussmaul breathing is the respiratory compensation blowing off CO₂ in metabolic acidosis (e.g. DKA).

questions.stem: In diffuse oesophageal spasm, NOT observed
questions.explanation: Reduced ganglia is achalasia; DES has normal ganglia.

questions.stem: A cause of neonatal jaundice is NOT
questions.explanation: Physiologic neonatal jaundice is unconjugated (↑production, ↓uptake, ↓conjugation). Impaired excretion → conjugated/cholestatic, not the physiologic pattern.

questions.stem: Mechanisms NOT involved in diabetic nephropathy
questions.explanation: By elimination — the hemodynamic/RAAS/genetic options are core mechanisms. VEGF is more characteristically tied to retinopathy, though it is also implicated in nephropathy; examiner-dependent.

questions.stem: Factors NOT involved in diabetic complications
questions.explanation: Duration, glycaemic control, metabolic memory and heredity drive complications; biological sex is not an established independent factor.
K⁺ is the principal intracellular cation; it is reabsorbed largely proximally/in the loop, and aldosterone promotes K⁺ excretion.

questions.stem: Which is NOT a clinical sign of heart failure
questions.explanation: Displaced apex, ankle oedema, JVD and S3 are heart-failure signs; blood pressure is typically normal or low, not raised.
Triglycerides supply fatty acids to muscle; the brain uses glucose/ketones and reverse cholesterol transport is an HDL function.
Both retained solute (osmotic diuresis) and tubular resistance to ADH drive the polyuria after relief of obstruction.
Hypocapnic alkalosis causes paraesthesiae and lightheadedness with cerebral vasoconstriction — not hypercapnia or acidosis.
Hyperthyroidism raises metabolic heat production; hypothyroidism, malnutrition and alcohol predispose to hypothermia.

questions.stem: Thrombocytopenia from ineffective thrombopoiesis is NOT caused by
questions.explanation: Hypersplenism = sequestration/destruction, not a production defect.
About 80–90% of filtered bicarbonate is reclaimed in the proximal convoluted tubule.
Water is lost as dilute urine, driving compensatory polydipsia; serum vasopressin is low in central DI.

questions.stem: A cause of malabsorption in the mucosal phase is NOT
questions.explanation: Ischaemia is a transport/delivery problem; coeliac, parasites, Crohn's and drugs damage the mucosa.

questions.stem: NOT a pathophysiological mechanism in heart failure
questions.explanation: Heart failure activates RAAS/sympathetic tone → increased peripheral resistance; dilatation, stiff ventricle, high LVEDP and remodelling are the mechanisms.
RBC lifespan is 120 DAYS, not hours.

questions.stem: Which is NOT an inhibitor of haematopoiesis
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin stimulates erythropoiesis; TNF and interferons are inhibitory to haematopoiesis.

questions.stem: Heat loss is NOT caused by
questions.explanation: Vasoconstriction conserves heat; vasodilation, sweating and insensible respiratory losses dissipate it.

questions.stem: NOT a characteristic finding of nephrotic syndrome
questions.explanation: Dysmorphic RBCs/casts indicate a nephritic (glomerular haematuria) picture; nephrotic = heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminaemia and oedema.
A high sodium load reliably raises blood pressure mainly in salt-sensitive individuals.
Disaccharidase deficiency is a brush-border/mucosal defect.

questions.stem: Easy fatigability in heart failure is due to
questions.explanation: Fatigue reflects low cardiac output and poor tissue perfusion, distinct from the congestive (dyspnoea) symptoms.
Normal sleep begins with NREM; the first REM period follows after roughly 90 minutes.

questions.stem: Features of acute inflammation include all EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Cardinal signs are redness, heat, swelling, pain and loss of function — the tissue is hyperaemic (red), not pale.

Best option: acromegalic cardiomyopathy is real ('in all patients' is an overstatement). Note: gigantism is paediatric; GH is diabetogenic (hyperglycaemia); it does affect joints/cartilage — so all options are imperfect.

questions.stem: NOT a vasoactive peptide of inflammation
questions.explanation: Histamine, serotonin and adenosine are vasoactive mediators; ferritin is an iron-storage/acute-phase protein, not vasoactive.

questions.stem: A cause of constipation is NOT
questions.explanation: Hypothyroidism causes constipation; hyperthyroidism tends to diarrhoea.

options.B: Urine Na may be > or < 20 by cause

questions.explanation: Renal loss gives urine Na >20 and extrarenal loss <20; adrenal insufficiency is a classic cause of hypovolaemic hyponatraemia.
Iron deficiency is microcytic; aplastic and sickle cell are normocytic and megaloblastic is macrocytic.

questions.stem: NOT a risk factor for coronary artery disease
questions.explanation: Established coronary risks are smoking, diabetes, hyperlipidaemia and family history; thyroid excess is not a classic risk factor.

questions.stem: NOT a risk factor for coronary artery disease
questions.explanation: As above — hyperthyroidism is not a conventional coronary risk factor.
Familial chylomicronaemia (type I) results from lipoprotein-lipase (or apoC-II) deficiency and carries a high pancreatitis risk.
Obesity hypoventilation features blunted central chemoresponsiveness plus reduced lung volumes, low chest-wall compliance and increased work of breathing.

questions.stem: Hyponatraemia with hypovolaemia is NOT caused by
questions.explanation: CKD gives hypervolaemic/euvolaemic hyponatraemia.
Fibrillin-1 mutation affects the eyes (lens dislocation), skeleton/joints, skin and — most dangerously — the aorta.
Effusions form from raised hydrostatic or permeability, low oncotic pressure, impaired drainage, or iatrogenic causes; the other listed options would reduce fluid.

questions.stem: The Mantoux test is an example of
questions.explanation: Delayed-type (T-cell-mediated) type IV hypersensitivity, like the Mantoux/tuberculin test.
'Cushing disease' specifically means a pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma; the circadian rhythm is lost and adrenal androgens rise.
Lipoproteins are lipids packaged with apolipoproteins (plus a phospholipid/cholesterol shell).

options.B: Supplied by nutrient vessels from bone

questions.explanation: The chondron (chondrocyte plus its pericellular matrix) is the functional unit; cartilage is avascular and histologically unlike bone.

questions.stem: Characteristic histology of rapidly progressive GN
questions.explanation: Rapidly progressive (crescentic) GN shows glomerular crescents; Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules are diabetic and basement-membrane thickening is membranous.

options.A: Diabetogenic genes cause resistance but not secretory defect
options.B: Risk not affected by family history
options.C: Genes cause resistance AND impaired secretion
options.D: More frequent in residents of Corsica
options.E: Genetic effect smaller than in T1DM

questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes genetics contribute to both insulin resistance and β-cell secretory failure, with a strong family-history effect.

questions.stem: NOT a characteristic finding of nephrotic syndrome
questions.explanation: Dysmorphic RBCs are a nephritic (haematuria) feature; nephrotic syndrome = proteinuria >3.5 g/24h, hypoalbuminaemia and oedema.
Factor V Leiden is inherited; nephrotic syndrome, antiphospholipid syndrome and myeloproliferative disorders are acquired prothrombotic states.
Best option (acromegalic cardiomyopathy). Other options are false — gigantism is paediatric, GH is diabetogenic, joints/cartilage are affected.
Pro-inflammatory cytokines cause fever, anorexia, catabolism and tachycardia/somnolence — they drive, not suppress, inflammation.
Autoimmune (type 1A) diabetes clusters with other organ-specific autoimmunity, classically Hashimoto (autoimmune) thyroiditis.

options.D: Acute pancreatitis is among its causes

questions.explanation: Third-spacing and vomiting cause fluid loss and shock; proximal obstruction → alkalosis and distal → acidosis (options a/c are reversed).

questions.stem: A cause of neonatal jaundice is NOT
questions.explanation: Impaired excretion → conjugated/cholestatic jaundice, not the unconjugated physiologic pattern.

questions.stem: Hyponatraemia with hypovolaemia is NOT caused by
questions.explanation: CKD produces euvolaemic/hypervolaemic hyponatraemia, not the hypovolaemic pattern.

questions.stem: Mechanisms NOT involved in diabetic nephropathy
questions.explanation: By elimination; examiner-dependent (VEGF is also implicated in nephropathy).

questions.stem: In subtotal gastrectomy, absorption is NOT impaired for
questions.explanation: Gastrectomy classically impairs iron (acid) and B12 (intrinsic factor). Fat digestion depends on pancreas/bile, so plain fats are keyed as 'not impaired' — but fat malabsorption can occur, so examiner-dependent.
Type 2 diabetes is a classic secondary cause of dyslipidaemia (high triglycerides, low HDL).
The standard type 1 diabetes autoantibodies are anti-islet (ICA), anti-insulin (IAA) and anti-GAD65.

questions.stem: Features of acute inflammation include all EXCEPT
questions.explanation: Inflamed tissue is red and hyperaemic; pallor is not a cardinal sign of acute inflammation.

questions.stem: Which is incorrect — cells/tissues are damaged by
questions.explanation: Injury results from deprivation, not surfeit, of oxygen/nutrients; mechanical, chemical and infectious causes are all valid.
Hyperglycaemia osmotically shifts water into plasma, diluting sodium (translocational/pseudo-hyponatraemia); correct ~1.6 mmol/L Na per 5.5 mmol/L glucose.

options.A: May occur in stress and hypothyroidism
options.C: Does not affect other pituitary hormones

questions.explanation: Stress, hypothyroidism (TRH excess), pregnancy and dopamine antagonists all raise prolactin — not always a prolactinoma; it suppresses gonadotropins and causes galactorrhoea.
By elimination — achalasia has aperistalsis with simultaneous contractions on manometry; LES tone is increased (relaxation fails); it is neuronal, not muscular.
TNF-α, IL-1 and IL-6 are the principal pro-inflammatory cytokines.

questions.stem: An initial finding in acute PE is NOT
questions.explanation: Early PE causes hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis and hypocapnia with hypoxaemia, not acidosis.
Hypothyroidism raises LDL/total cholesterol and is a classic secondary dyslipidaemia.

options.A: Upper regions hypoventilated relative to perfusion
options.B: Perfusion at bases half that at apices
options.C: Upper regions hyperventilated relative to perfusion
options.D: Perfusion at bases double that at apices

questions.explanation: At the apex ventilation exceeds perfusion (high V/Q); perfusion is greatest at the bases.

questions.stem: Thrombocytopenia from ineffective thrombopoiesis is NOT caused by
questions.explanation: Hypersplenism destroys/sequesters platelets rather than impairing their production.

questions.stem: A cause of hyperkalaemia is NOT
questions.explanation: Aldosteronism (primary or secondary) causes hypokalaemia.
Antibody-mediated red-cell destruction is a type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity reaction.
Respiratory alkalosis lowers ionised calcium and constricts cerebral vessels → paraesthesiae and confusion.
Sleep onset is NREM; the first REM period follows after roughly 90 minutes.

questions.stem: Main pathophysiological mechanisms in heart failure
questions.explanation: Heart failure involves dilatation with reduced contractility, reduced compliance, raised LVEDP and remodelling — all of the listed mechanisms.
Angiotensin II is a vasoconstrictor that stimulates thirst, ADH, sympathetic tone and sodium retention.

questions.stem: Which are NOT mature blood cells
questions.explanation: Myelocytes are immature precursors; neutrophils and RBCs are mature circulating cells.
Aldosterone excess drives renal H⁺ and K⁺ loss → hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis.
Vomiting/NG loss of gastric HCl generates metabolic alkalosis; the other options cause metabolic acidosis.
Hypercalcaemia shortens QT; PTH may be low (malignancy).
NSAIDs block afferent-arteriolar vasodilatory prostaglandins → reduced renal perfusion and GFR.

questions.stem: A cause of malabsorption in the mucosal phase is NOT
questions.explanation: Coeliac is the only mucosal-phase option. Zollinger-Ellison and bacterial overgrowth are intraluminal and ischaemia is transport-phase — so the stem has multiple non-mucosal options; ZE keyed as the classic intraluminal example.

questions.stem: Causes of decreased ventilation do NOT include

options.A: Stimulation of respiratory centre e.g. progesterone

questions.explanation: Keyed: stimulation increases ventilation. Pneumonia (parenchymal, type 1) is also defensible.
Lipoproteins carry lipids bound to apolipoproteins.
A high sodium load raises blood pressure mainly in salt-sensitive individuals.

options.A: Skin and physical barriers (mucus, cilia, hair)

questions.explanation: Barriers such as skin, mucus, cilia and hair are innate; B-lymphocytes and plasma cells belong to adaptive immunity.
Iron deficiency is the classic microcytic, hypochromic anaemia.

questions.stem: Sickle cell disease is due to
questions.explanation: A single β-globin point mutation produces HbS, which polymerises when deoxygenated; it is not a quantitative chain-synthesis (thalassaemia) defect.
LPS/endotoxin is an exogenous pyrogen; IL-1, TNF and interferons are endogenous pyrogens.

questions.stem: Dyspnoea in heart failure is due to
questions.explanation: Raised filling pressure transmits to the pulmonary capillaries, causing congestion and breathlessness.

questions.stem: NOT a risk factor for coronary artery disease
questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism is not a conventional coronary risk factor, unlike smoking, diabetes, hyperlipidaemia and family history.
Iatrogenic causes are valid; decreased permeability, decreased hydrostatic and increased oncotic pressure would each reduce, not create, an effusion.
Innate immunity is fast and non-specific with no memory; it is present across many organisms (not only mammals) and lacks fine antigen specificity.
Acute coronary syndromes span unstable angina, NSTEMI and STEMI.

questions.stem: NOT a cause of heart failure
questions.explanation: Athletic remodelling is physiological; the other options are pathological causes of heart failure.

options.A: Acid secretion usually normal or decreased

questions.explanation: Duodenal ulcer = increased acid, so 'normal/decreased' is the false statement.
Membranous GN, minimal change disease and diabetic nephropathy all cause nephrotic-range proteinuria.
Nitrosamines are classic dietary β-cell toxins. Early cow's-milk exposure and short breastfeeding are also cited environmental triggers — examiner-dependent.
